content.text: without waiting for an external stimulus to tell it to act. Rather than sitting idle until something happens in the environment, a proactive agent actively plans steps, executes them, monitors progress, and revises plans when necessary.

Proactivity is the complement to reactivity. Reactive agents respond to what the environment does to them. Proactive agents drive toward what they want to achieve in the environment. Most useful agents need both: they respond to unexpected events (reactivity) while also pursuing long-horizon goals (proactivity).
The AOSE (Agent-Oriented Software Engineering) definition: an agent is proactive if it exhibits
by taking the initiative rather than acting purely in response to its environment.

Proactive agents operate on a cycle: form or receive a goal, make a plan, execute the plan, and monitor whether the goal has been achieved. If progress stalls or the plan fails, they replan and retry.

attrs.alt: Proactivity: goal-plan-execute-monitor cycle vs reactive-only agent
attrs.title: Proactivity in AI Agents

content.text: A purely reactive agent (left column) does nothing without a trigger. The proactive agent (centre) runs its own loop: it holds a goal, makes a plan, executes steps, and monitors progress — looping until the goal is met or it decides to abandon the goal.
The simplest form of proactivity is a
: the agent keeps acting until a goal test returns true.

Many production proactive agents work through scheduling: they set up future work themselves rather than waiting to be told. A monitoring agent that checks metrics every five minutes is proactive — it drives the cadence, not an external trigger.
LLM-based agents exhibit proactivity through the
of each ReAct iteration: the model decides which tool to call next based on the current goal, not because an external event fired. The agent is driving the action sequence forward.
: given a high-level goal, the agent breaks it into sub-tasks and pursues them in order without waiting for the user to specify each step.
: before starting a task, the agent proactively gathers context it anticipates needing (reading a config file, checking the current environment, fetching recent docs).
: while executing one goal, the agent notices a related problem and fixes it without being asked (e.g., fixing a lint error it spotted while implementing a feature).
Proactivity has a failure mode: an agent that is
proactive takes actions the user did not want, fixing things that weren't broken or surfacing unsolicited information that creates noise rather than value.
Three constraints keep proactivity from becoming intrusiveness:
: only take initiative within the explicitly defined domain. A code review agent should not proactively rewrite tests that were not mentioned in the task.
: when acting on initiative (no explicit user request), prefer reversible actions. Avoid irreversible proactive actions without explicit authorisation.
: report proactive actions taken. A user should always be able to see what the agent did on its own initiative and why.
A proactive agent may have multiple active goals. It needs to decide which to pursue and when to suspend one goal to handle another. Three priority strategies:
Prioritise by time remaining until deadline
Weigh goal value against estimated effort
Complex multi-goal agents with resource constraints
Proactivity and autonomy are related but distinct concepts:
: does the agent take action without being prompted, or does it wait for a stimulus?
: does the agent act without human approval, or does it ask before each action?

A proactive agent can still operate at Level 2 autonomy (Human-in-Loop): it proactively drafts a plan and proposes actions, but waits for human approval before executing each one. Proactivity says who initiates the work; autonomy says who approves the execution.

One of the most common forms of proactivity in LLM agents is pre-task information gathering: before starting a task, the agent reads context it predicts will be needed. This reduces back-and-forth and prevents mid-task blocking when required context is missing.
Different patterns of proactive behaviour suit different deployment scenarios:
Before starting a new task, without being asked
When a related issue is noticed during another task
Fixing lint errors spotted while implementing
When the agent infers that a future goal is likely

A genuinely proactive agent does not give up when a plan step fails. It replans: diagnoses the failure, adjusts the plan, and continues. This replanning loop is what separates a robust proactive agent from a fragile script.
Proactivity is goal-directed behaviour: the agent takes initiative to achieve goals rather than waiting for external stimuli.
The proactive cycle is: set/receive goal → make plan → execute steps → monitor progress → repeat or revise.
LLM agents exhibit proactivity through task decomposition, anticipatory context-gathering, and opportunistic sub-tasking.
Over-proactivity is a real failure mode: bound proactive behaviour by scope, prefer reversible actions, and log all self-initiated steps.
Multi-goal agents need a priority strategy (fixed, deadline, or expected-value) to decide which goal to pursue when several are active.
Proactivity (who initiates) is orthogonal to autonomy (who approves): a proactive agent can still require human confirmation before each action.
